skill.name: self-test
skill.description: Guides agents through tests, lint, and type checks while implementing code so issues are fixed before completion.
skill.summary: Use a repeatable self-test loop before declaring code complete.
skill.author: Clouder0

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged shell command examples, a weak cryptography pattern, and a network reconnaissance pattern in SKILL.md. Review found the shell references are documentation examples for local tests, linting, and type checks, while the other patterns have no supporting semantic evidence. The skill still requests Bash access, so users should run project commands only in trusted workspaces.

low_findings.title: Documented Shell Command Examples
low_findings.description: The flagged locations are fenced documentation blocks that show local test, lint, and type-check commands. They do not execute automatically, but Bash access can run project-specific commands when a user invokes the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are code fence boundaries and command examples in prose. No script file, automatic execution hook, or command interpolation is present in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The flagged description line contains normal prose about implementation quality. No hashing, encryption, signature, or cryptographic API usage is present at the cited location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is YAML metadata text and contains no cryptographic algorithm reference. The surrounding file content also shows no cryptographic workflow.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Pattern Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The flagged checklist item says all tests should pass. It does not describe network scanning, host discovery, port probing, or external requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 75 is a plain checklist item about test status. No network command or reconnaissance intent appears in the reviewed skill file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Permission With Local Command Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill metadata allows Bash and the instructions recommend running local project commands. This is expected for a self-testing workflow, but command execution should remain limited to trusted repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Bash is explicitly allowed and the documented commands are local developer tooling. The risk is operational rather than evidence of malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Code changes can look complete while tests, lint, or type checks still fail. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a repeatable loop for testing work during implementation.
actual_capabilities: Guides iterative testing while code is being implemented.
Prompts the agent to run project-specific test commands.
Includes common examples for npm, pytest, TypeScript, ESLint, mypy, and Ruff.
Defines a pre-completion checklist for tests, lint, type checks, and console warnings.
Encourages immediate fixes when verification steps fail.
limitations: Does not provide its own test runner or lint configuration.
Depends on commands already available in the target project.
May require user approval before Bash commands run in restricted environments.
Does not guarantee full coverage for untested business logic.

use_cases.title: Validate a Feature Change
use_cases.description: Run focused tests, lint, and type checks while implementing a new feature.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Pull Request
use_cases.description: Confirm that required verification steps pass before asking for review.

use_cases.title: Stabilize Generated Code
use_cases.description: Make an assistant fix failures immediately instead of reporting incomplete work as finished.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Basic Self-Test
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner verification after a small edit
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the self-test loop on my latest change. Run the relevant project tests and report any failures before continuing.

prompt_templates.title: Check Tests and Lint
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate verification for a changed file set
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the self-test workflow to the files I changed. Run focused tests, lint, and type checks where the project supports them.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Failures Immediately
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced repair loop for failing verification
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the self-test loop until the implementation is ready. When a test, lint, or type check fails, fix the cause and rerun the affected command.

prompt_templates.title: Gate Completion on Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Before marking the work complete, use this skill to verify tests, lint, type checks, and console output. Summarize the commands and results.

output_examples.input: Self-test the authentication change before completion.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports the focused authentication tests, lint result, and type-check result, then lists one fixed failure before final status.

output_examples.input: Use the self-test loop on the modified Python module.
output_examples.output: The assistant runs the relevant Python tests and static checks, fixes reported issues, and states which verification steps passed.

output_examples.input: Confirm this generated code is ready for review.
output_examples.output: The assistant refuses to mark the work complete until tests, lint, type checks, and console checks have been addressed.

best_practices: Run the smallest relevant test first, then broaden verification when risk increases.
Fix failures as soon as they appear and rerun the affected command.
Report exact verification status instead of assuming the work is complete.
anti_patterns: Declaring completion before running available tests.
Batching all verification until the end of a large implementation.
Ignoring lint, type-check, or console failures because the main test passed.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides an assistant to test code during implementation and fix failures before completion.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: It provides command guidance for the assistant. Actual execution depends on the tool environment and user permissions.

faq.question: Which languages does it support?
faq.answer: It includes examples for JavaScript, TypeScript, and Python, but it can use any project-specific test workflow.

faq.question: Can it replace project tests?
faq.answer: No. It depends on the tests, linters, and type checkers already configured in the project.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it while implementing code, before pull requests, and before an assistant marks work as complete.

faq.question: What is the main security consideration?
faq.answer: The skill uses Bash-capable workflows, so commands should run only in trusted repositories and reviewed environments.
